meta.title: Type Safety First: Contracts & Validation at Every Boundary
meta.excerpt: Learn how Zod schemas and TypeScript contracts validate data at every layer of our architecture, catching bugs at compile time and runtime.

seo.metaTitle: Type Safety First: Contracts & Validation — TypeScript Best Practices
seo.metaDescription: Discover how to use Zod schemas and TypeScript contracts to validate data at every boundary of your application, preventing bugs before they happen.
seo.keywords: type safety, typescript, zod, schema validation, contracts, compile-time validation, runtime validation

toc.title: Example: Article Flow Through All Boundaries

blocks.content: The Type Safety Philosophy
Type safety is our primary defense against bugs. By validating data at compile time with TypeScript and at runtime with Zod, we catch entire categories of bugs before they reach production.
The principle is simple: data should be validated as close to its source as possible, and never trusted until validated. This means:

items.title: API data is untrusted
items.description: Even data from our own Strapi API might be malformed or changed unexpectedly

items.title: Every transformation is a contract
items.description: When data moves from layer to layer, we validate it matches the expected shape

items.title: TypeScript is not enough
items.description: We need runtime validation (Zod) because TypeScript types are erased at runtime

items.title: Fail fast and explicitly
items.description: If data is invalid, the app fails immediately with a clear error, not silently with undefined values

blocks.content: Contracts at Every Boundary
Our architecture has four critical boundaries where data crosses layer borders. Each boundary has explicit validation:
Boundary 1: API → DTO
DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) represent the exact shape of data coming from external sources. Zod schemas validate API responses at import time.
If the API returns unexpected data (missing field, wrong type), the parse fails with a clear error message. No silent undefined bugs.
Boundary 2: DTO → Domain
Mappers transform DTOs into Domain Models. The Domain Model is our internal representation, enriched with computed properties.
The TypeScript compiler ensures the Domain Model is returned with all required fields. Missing a property is a compile error.
Boundary 3: Repository → Page
Repositories define interfaces that specify which methods exist and what they return. Pages call repository methods with confidence.
If you try to call a method that doesn't exist, TypeScript catches it immediately. And the return type is always known.
Boundary 4: Page → Component
Pages pass view models as props to components. TypeScript enforces that all required props are provided with correct types.
Example: Article Flow Through All Boundaries
Here's how a single article flows through all four boundaries with validation at each step:

items.title: DTO schema validates shape
items.description: ArticleDTOSchema.parse() ensures exact API shape matches

items.title: Mapper transforms to Domain
items.description: mapArticleDTO() converts to Article with computed properties, TypeScript ensures all fields present

items.title: Repository method returns typed result
items.description: articleRepository.getBySlug() returns Promise<Article | null>, interface contract is enforced

items.title: Page receives repository result
items.description: Article is guaranteed to have id, title, slug, content, excerpt, readTime, publishedAt; no undefined surprises

items.title: Component receives props with type checking
items.description: ArticleView component receives article prop that matches ArticleViewProps, TypeScript enforces all required props

blocks.content: Why Type Safety Matters
Type safety prevents entire classes of bugs:

items.title: Undefined reference errors
items.description: Impossible to access a property that doesn't exist because TypeScript knows the shape of every object

items.title: API contract changes
items.description: When an external API changes, Zod validation fails immediately with a clear error, not silently with wrong data

items.title: Prop mismatches
items.description: Passing the wrong props to a component is caught at build time, not discovered at runtime in production

items.title: Impossible states
items.description: TypeScript prevents you from creating invalid states (e.g., an article without a slug)

items.title: Refactoring confidence
items.description: When you rename a field or change a shape, the compiler tells you everywhere that needs updating

blocks.title: Result: Bugs Caught Before Production
blocks.content: With contracts at every boundary and validation at every layer, most bugs are caught during development or at build time. Your code reaches production with confidence.
